images.original_text:
中古國伏劍法
編者：唐蒙
武藝叢書第二輯之一

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot slightly advanced, right hand gripping a sword held horizontally at waist level, left hand extended forward in a guiding gesture. Body angled slightly to the left, weight balanced on both legs with emphasis on the front leg for readiness.
persons.tactic: Preparatory guard position for close-quarters sword combat, signaling readiness to parry or counterattack.

persons.posture: Mounted on horseback, leaning forward with right arm raised holding a spear pointed diagonally upward. Left hand grips reins; body twisted slightly to align spear with target. Weight shifted forward over horse’s neck for momentum.
persons.tactic: Rapid charge attack using mounted spear thrust, designed to disrupt enemy formation or strike vulnerable flank.

persons.posture: Kneeling on one knee, left arm extended backward for balance, right arm drawing a bowstring back to cheek. Eyes focused forward, feet planted wide apart for stability. Torso upright with slight lean into draw.
persons.tactic: Archery shot from kneeling position—maximizing accuracy and concealment while maintaining power through body alignment.

persons.posture: Standing beside chariot wheel, holding a halberd with both hands in a two-handed grip, blade angled downward. Feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent slightly for shock absorption. Head turned toward opponent’s left side.
persons.tactic: Defensive blocking or sweeping strike against charging cavalry, leveraging weapon length and leverage for control.

persons.posture: Seated inside chariot, holding reins with left hand, right hand resting near hip. Back straight, shoulders relaxed, gaze directed ahead. Chariot wheels visible behind, suggesting motion.
persons.tactic: Commander or driver role—maintaining vehicle control while observing battlefield dynamics for tactical deployment.

persons.posture: On horseback, facing rearward, right arm swinging a sword in circular arc above head. Left arm bent, hand near chest for balance. Legs braced against saddle, torso rotating with swing.
persons.tactic: Retreating slash or counter-attack maneuver, using momentum of horse movement to generate cutting force against pursuers.

persons.posture: Standing with feet apart, left leg forward, right leg back in a horse stance. Right hand holds a short staff vertically near shoulder, left hand open and extended outward. Torso slightly twisted to right.
persons.tactic: Staff defense against incoming strikes, preparing for simultaneous offensive follow-up via rotational pivot.

persons.posture: Reclining on ground, one leg bent, other extended. Right arm raised holding a small dagger near face, left hand pressing down on ground for support. Head tilted upward, eyes fixed on attacker above.
persons.tactic: Last-ditch defensive technique—using terrain and low profile to evade or stab upward when cornered, emphasizing surprise and precision.

images.original_text:
中古國俠劍法
編 樂 唐
武藝二精之一書
中古國俠劍法

persons.posture: The figure is in a dynamic martial stance, with one leg forward and the other back for balance. The body is slightly crouched, torso angled forward, weight distributed with more pressure on the front leg. The right arm extends forward holding a sword horizontally at chest level, blade pointing left; the left arm is bent and held close to the body, palm facing inward. The head is turned slightly toward the direction of the sword’s point, eyes focused ahead. This posture suggests a preparatory or offensive position in sword combat, possibly a thrust or parry setup.
persons.tactic: This appears to depict a classic sword technique from classical Chinese martial arts, likely a foundational stance for offense or defense. The horizontal sword position indicates readiness to strike or block, consistent with traditional sword forms emphasizing control, precision, and linear movement.

images.original_text:
武藝叢書自序
揚棄了俺！作一頁歌的整理和估價。更妥貼明的是：武藝本身在人類歷史進展上，目前以至將來的社會，他應該佔有怎樣的地位和價值？這使明瞭了以後，我們纔能得到一個正確的，帶有科學性的管見標準，纔不至於盲目的努力，從實踐中可以更接近地證明了所謂武藝的價值在哪裏？所以，最後吾打算簡納各種武藝資料，來撰作一部中國武藝統論，以謂當天兄上達這個厚望；如其環境容許的話，或者寫寫一部內容比較充實的中國武藝綜論也未可知？一九·六年七月一日·唐榮。

目
四 狗頭勢
五 扭腹勢
六 路右勢
七 撩拔勢
八 御車勢
九 展旗勢
一〇 看守勢
一一 銀蟒勢
一二 鎮壓勢
一三 廢擊勢
一四 展翅勢
一五 右翼勢

丑圖勢
集鼎勢
舉鼎勢者：即擊鼎格也。【法】能擋格上教，左闢右手掙撥勢，向前擊中殺，【武備志左腳右手年接，勢向前，掣擊甲殺】予參考他勢反其用法，改為兩句。
點劍勢
點劍擊者：即點劍側也。【法】能偏閃疾進搶殺，右腳右手撥草尋起勢，向前前步御車格，看法
中國古失劍法

persons.posture: Stance: wide-legged, knees bent, body slightly crouched; left hand holds sword hilt near waist, right arm extended forward with sword tip pointing ahead; weight distributed evenly on both feet, torso angled slightly forward for balance and readiness to advance or parry.
persons.tactic: Executing a 'pointing sword' technique (點劍勢), advancing with controlled footwork while maintaining defensive posture. The movement is designed to strike at an opponent’s midsection or limbs while simultaneously preparing for counterattack or evasion.

persons.posture: Stance: forward lunge with right leg extended, left knee bent low; both hands gripping sword hilt above head in a vertical guard position, sword blade pointing upward; body leans back slightly to maintain balance under the force of the raised weapon; weight primarily on rear leg to allow explosive forward thrust or downward strike.
persons.tactic: Performing a 'lifting and striking' maneuver (集鼎勢), using the sword as a vertical shield or preparatory strike against overhead attacks. This posture suggests readiness to either block incoming blows or deliver a powerful downward slash upon opponent’s exposed upper body.

images.original_text:
中國古佚劍法
左翼勢
左翼勢者，即左翼擊也。一法
能上挑下壓，直殺虎口，右腳右手直符送書勢，向前舉步這鰲刺，看法。
豹頭勢
豹頭勢者，即豹頭擊也。一法
能露膝上段，左手牽山壓頂勢，向前舉步挑刺，看法。

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot forward, body slightly crouched. Both hands grip a sword held high above the head, angled downward as if preparing to strike. Weight is distributed with more pressure on the front (left) leg, back (right) leg bent and supporting. The sword is positioned for an overhead slash or thrust toward the opponent’s upper body or head.
persons.tactic: Left Wing Technique — A striking motion using the sword to attack from above, targeting the opponent’s throat or upper torso while maintaining balance and readiness to follow up with a step forward.

persons.posture: In a wide, grounded stance with right foot forward, body turned slightly sideways. Sword held horizontally across the shoulders, arms extended backward, ready to swing forward in a powerful horizontal cut. Weight is balanced between both legs, knees bent for stability. The posture suggests preparation for a sweeping or chopping strike.
persons.tactic: Leopard Head Technique — A low-to-high striking maneuver using the sword to deliver a forceful blow from shoulder height, targeting the opponent’s midsection or head, often following a step forward to close distance.

images.original_text:
扭腹勢
扭腹勢者，即扭頸刺也。法能沖刺中殺，邁如崩山；右腳右手蓄龍出水勢，向前進步壓擊，法。
跨右勢
跨右勢者，即跨右擊也。法能撩剪下殺，左腳右手繞衣勢，向前進步橫擊，看法。
序圖·古俠劍法

persons.posture: Left foot forward in a straddle stance, body slightly crouched with weight shifted to front leg. Right hand holds sword extended forward in a horizontal cutting motion, left hand positioned near waist for balance. Torso angled slightly forward, eyes focused on target.
persons.tactic: Executing a 'Cross-Right Strike' technique — advancing with the right foot while delivering a horizontal slash with the sword, aiming to cut downward or across the opponent’s midsection.

persons.posture: Right foot forward in a wide stance, body leaning back slightly with weight distributed evenly. Left hand grips sword hilt high near shoulder, right arm extended forward in a thrusting posture. Head turned sideways, gaze fixed ahead.
persons.tactic: Performing a 'Twist-Hip Strike' — using rotational hip movement to generate power for a thrusting attack, simulating a piercing strike aimed at the opponent’s centerline or throat area.

images.original_text:
中國古俠劍法
接掠勢
能逃掠者：即旋掠各也。『法』
能避下殺，蔽左膝右，左腳左手長較分水勢，向前單步鐗擊，看法。
御車勢
御車勢者：卽御車格也。『法』
能御鐗中殺，削敵雙手，左腳右手沖鋒勢，向前退步鳳頭洗，看法。

persons.posture: Stance: forward step with left foot, body slightly crouched, weight shifted forward. Right hand holds sword extended horizontally in front, left hand positioned near hip or abdomen for balance. Arms are extended to block or strike. Body angle is angled slightly to the right, facing opponent. Weight distribution: 60% on front (left) leg, 40% on rear (right) leg.
persons.tactic: Executing a 'Jie Lüe Shi' (Escape and Sweep Technique), using a horizontal sword block to deflect an incoming attack while preparing to counter with a forward thrust or cut.

persons.posture: Stance: left foot forward, right foot back in a low horse stance. Body is turned sideways, knees bent deeply. Sword held high above shoulder with both hands, blade pointing downward diagonally. Torso leans forward slightly, eyes focused ahead. Weight distribution: 70% on front (left) leg, 30% on rear (right) leg.
persons.tactic: Performing a 'Yu Che Shi' (Defense Against Chariot Attack Technique), using a high guard to intercept an overhead strike while preparing to descend with a powerful downward slash or parry.

images.original_text:
展旗勢
展旗勢者：即展旗擊也。一法能剪腳上殺；左腳左手托落勢，向前履步點劍，看法。
看守勢
看守勢者：即看守擊也。一法能看守落勢，攻則守定，諸器兼進，相機隨勢滾殺，左腿右手虎踏勢，向前進步威擊，看法。
中國古佚劍法

persons.posture: Left figure in a low, forward-leaning stance with left foot forward and right foot back. Left hand holds a staff horizontally at waist level, right hand is raised near shoulder. Body is angled slightly forward, weight distributed mostly on front leg, ready to advance or strike.
persons.tactic: Executing the 'Zhan Qi Shi' (Displaying Banner Technique), using a staff to control distance while preparing for a forward thrust or cut. The posture suggests readiness to follow up with a step and point of the sword as described in the text.

persons.posture: Right figure in a dynamic advancing stance with right foot forward and left foot trailing. Right hand grips a sword pointed upward diagonally, left hand holds a staff vertically beside the body. Torso is upright but leaning slightly forward, weight balanced for immediate movement or counterattack.
persons.tactic: Performing the 'Kan Shou Shi' (Watching Guard Technique), maintaining defensive posture while preparing to strike with the sword. The positioning indicates readiness to respond to an opponent’s attack by stepping forward and executing a precise cut or thrust.

images.original_text:
中國古俠劍法
銀蟬勢
銀蟬者：即銀蟬布也。一法四顧周身，又能按殺四面，向前則左手左腳，向後則右手右腳，前則左右旋風，掣電般，看法。
鑽擊勢
鑽擊者：即鑽擊也。一法能鑽格擒敵，鴟形鴉步奔沖，左腳左手白猿出洞勢，向前掣步壓擊，看法。

persons.posture: Stance: feet apart, left foot forward; body slightly crouched, weight shifted forward; arms raised high above head holding sword in both hands, blade angled upward; head turned right, eyes focused forward. This is the 'Silver Cicada' posture, preparing for a sweeping or downward strike.
persons.tactic: The technique involves a four-directional guard (四顧周身) to defend all sides, then advancing with coordinated hand and foot movement (前則左手左腳，向後則右手右腳). The motion resembles wind and lightning (旋風，掣電般), indicating rapid, fluid transitions. The 'Drill Strike' (鑽擊勢) is a low, aggressive charge using a crow-like stance (鴟形鴉步) to pierce through defenses with a sudden thrusting attack.

persons.posture: Stance: feet together, body upright, weight balanced; sword held horizontally across chest with both hands, blade pointing left; head turned left, eyes focused on opponent. This is the preparatory or defensive phase of the 'Silver Cicada' posture, ready to counter or advance.
persons.tactic: This figure demonstrates the same 'Silver Cicada' posture as the left person, but from the opposite side — guarding against attacks from the front and rear by shifting weight between hands and feet. The 'Drill Strike' is not yet initiated here, suggesting a moment of readiness before launching an offensive move.

images.original_text:
腰擊勢
腰擊勢者，即應擊也。法能橫沖中敵，身步手劍疾若迅雷，此一擊者，劍中之首擊也。右腳右手斬蛇勢，向前進步近陣，看法。
展翅勢
展翅勢者，即展翅擊也。法能綴格上殺，撩按下擊，右腳右手偏因勢，向前擊步聚指，看法。

persons.posture: Stance: Forward advancing step with right foot, body slightly crouched and angled forward. Right hand holds sword in a high slashing position (as if cutting down), left hand extended backward for balance. Weight distributed primarily on the front leg, ready to strike or follow through.
persons.tactic: Executing a '腰擊勢' (Wa Ji Shi) — a powerful midsection strike using the sword, initiated by a rapid forward advance with the right foot, combining speed and force as described in the text.

persons.posture: Stance: Low crouch with both knees bent, body leaning forward slightly. Sword held horizontally across the front, blade pointing forward, ready to slash downward or parry. Both feet planted wide for stability, weight centered low for power generation.
persons.tactic: Performing a '展翅勢' (Zhan Chi Shi) — a wing-like expansion technique involving a sweeping downward cut from an elevated position, followed by a forward thrusting motion to close distance and attack the opponent’s centerline.

images.original_text:
右翼勢
右翼勢者：即右翼擊也。一法能剪敵兩翼，左腳右手圓字勢，向前舉步騰躍，看法。
揭擊勢
揭擊勢者：刨揭擊也。一法龍勢格上殺，步步套進，左腳左手虎坐勢，向前退步沖洗，看法。

persons.posture: Left foot forward in a low stance, right foot back; body slightly crouched with weight on front leg; left hand raised in a circular guard near the head, right hand holding sword extended forward in a thrusting motion; torso angled slightly to the right, eyes focused forward.
persons.tactic: Right Wing Strike — advancing with a sweeping cut to the opponent’s flank while maintaining balance and readiness for follow-up strikes.

persons.posture: Right foot forward in a wide horse stance, left foot back; body upright with weight evenly distributed; both hands gripping sword in a high guard position above the head, arms bent at elbows; facing forward with alert posture.
persons.tactic: Right Wing Strike — preparing to strike downward or diagonally with the sword while controlling distance and positioning for counterattack.

images.original_text:
右翼勢
右翼勢者：即右翼擊也。一法能剪敵兩翼，左腳右手圓字勢，向前舉步騰躍，看法。
揭擊勢
揭擊勢者：刨揭擊也。一法龍勢格上殺，步步套進，左腳左手虎坐勢，向前退步沖洗，看法。

persons.posture: Left foot forward in a low stance, right foot back; body slightly crouched with weight on front leg; left hand raised in a circular guard near the head, right hand holding sword extended forward in a thrusting motion; torso angled slightly to the right, eyes focused forward.
persons.tactic: Right Wing Strike — advancing with a sweeping cut to the opponent’s flank while maintaining balance and readiness for follow-up strikes.

persons.posture: Right foot forward in a wide horse stance, left foot back; body upright with weight evenly distributed; both hands gripping sword in a high guard position above the head, arms bent at elbows; facing forward with alert posture.
persons.tactic: Right Wing Strike — preparing to strike downward or diagonally with the sword while controlling distance and positioning for counterattack.

images.original_text:
左夾勢
左夾勢者：即左夾劍也。法能沖刺中敵，右腳右手壓頭勢；伺機進步壓擊，承法。
（註：原圖誤植反背，應正面執劍方合，參看右夾劍自明。）
跨左勢
跨左勢者：即跨左擊也。法能掃拔下殺，右腿右手提水勢，向前遊步變剪，承法。

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot forward, right foot back. Body slightly crouched, weight distributed forward. Right hand holds sword pointing diagonally upward, left hand positioned near the hip or abdomen, palm facing inward. The sword is held with the blade angled upward and outward, ready for a thrusting or cutting motion.
persons.tactic: Executing the 'Left夹势' (Left Clamp Posture), which involves advancing with the left foot to strike the opponent's midsection while using the right hand to control or pressure the opponent’s head or upper body. This technique combines offensive thrusting with defensive control.

persons.posture: In a wide, low stance with right foot forward and left foot back. Body turned sideways, weight shifted onto the front leg. Both arms are raised, holding a sword with the blade pointed upward and slightly backward, as if preparing to parry or counterattack. The head is turned toward the opponent, eyes focused on the target.
persons.tactic: Performing the 'Cross Left Posture' (跨左勢), which involves stepping across with the left leg to deliver a sweeping or downward cut while maintaining balance and readiness to follow up with another strike. The movement emphasizes mobility and timing to disrupt the opponent’s defense.

images.original_text:
中國古俠劍法
抓擊勢
抓擊者：即抓擊，左腿抵搖上殺，搶步鐗殺，左腳右手朝天勢，向前退步扭服刺，看法。
逆鋒勢
逆鋒者：即造鋒刺也。一法能直刺喉頸，右腳右手採海勢，向前掣步左翼擊，看法。

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot forward and right foot back, body slightly crouched. Right hand grips the hilt of a sword held diagonally upward, left hand extended forward in a thrusting motion. Weight is distributed with more pressure on the front leg, torso angled slightly forward to generate momentum for a strike.
persons.tactic: Executing the '抓擊勢' (Grasp Strike Posture), advancing with a quick step to close distance and deliver a downward or diagonal slash using the sword, targeting the opponent’s throat or upper body.

persons.posture: In a side-stepping posture with right foot forward and left foot trailing. Body turned sideways, left arm extended holding a staff horizontally across the body, right arm bent at elbow with hand near shoulder, ready to strike or parry. Weight balanced between legs, knees slightly bent for stability and mobility.
persons.tactic: Performing the '逆鋒勢' (Reverse Edge Posture), using the staff to create a defensive or counter-attacking angle while preparing to thrust or sweep toward the opponent’s vulnerable areas, possibly targeting the neck or ribs.

images.original_text:
纵翅势
欲翅者，即欲翅也。法能佯北掳腾，左右脚接蛇势，倒退遁步履，看法。
右夹势
右夹势者：即右夹肘也。法能截塌中藏，左脚右手冲势，向前立步裹格，看法。

persons.posture: Stance: Forward-facing, left foot forward in a bow stance, right foot back. Body slightly crouched, weight distributed forward. Right hand holds staff horizontally across chest, left hand grips staff near tip, angled upward. Head turned slightly to right, eyes focused forward. Arms bent, elbows tucked close to body for defense and control.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Right夹势' (Right夹 Elbow Technique), where the practitioner uses a staff to intercept an opponent’s centerline while simultaneously advancing with a forward step. The left hand controls the staff’s tip to block or redirect incoming strikes, while the right hand provides leverage and stability. The movement combines defensive interception with offensive pressure.

persons.posture: Stance: Backward-facing, right foot back in a retreat stance, left foot forward. Body leaning backward slightly, weight shifted rearward. Staff held diagonally across body from upper right to lower left, tip pointing toward ground. Head turned left, eyes tracking the opponent. Arms extended but relaxed, ready to pivot or counterattack.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Zong Chi Shi' (Flying Wings Posture), a feigned retreat technique. The practitioner simulates fleeing to lure the opponent into overextending, then uses the staff to execute a sudden reversal — either a strike or trap using the staff’s length and leverage. The backward step allows for evasion while maintaining control of distance and timing.

images.original_text:
中國古佚劍法
鳳頭勢
鳳頭勢者：仰鳳頭也。【法能洗刺剪殺，右腳右手白蛇弄風勢，向前舉步揭擊；看法。
橫沖勢
橫沖勢者：刨橫沖擊也。【法能疾奔翻回！疑是問字。【漾紋，進退伸手兩腳隨勢，沖進聚】
疑是掣字。『步撩掠，看法。

persons.posture: Stance: Wide-legged, knees bent, body slightly crouched. Right hand grips staff resting horizontally across shoulders, left hand extended forward in a defensive or preparatory position. Feet are planted firmly, weight distributed evenly for stability. Body angled slightly forward, ready to advance or strike.
persons.tactic: Executing the 'Fengtou Shi' (Phoenix Head Posture), using the staff to create an overhead sweeping motion while preparing for a forward thrust or strike. The posture suggests a defensive maneuver transitioning into offense.

persons.posture: Stance: Forward-leaning, left leg advanced, right leg back for balance. Right hand holds sword raised diagonally above shoulder, blade pointing upward; left hand is extended forward, palm facing outward, possibly controlling distance or deflecting. Weight shifted onto front leg, indicating readiness to lunge or parry.
persons.tactic: Performing the 'Hengchong Shi' (Horizontal Rush Posture) with sword, aiming to cut downward or slice sideways while advancing. The posture implies a dynamic offensive technique combining footwork and blade control.

images.original_text:
中國古俠劍法跋
興撲採勢同。茲附日本劍道「構」圖於左，以資參証。
(一) 諸手上段之構

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot slightly forward. The figure holds a staff raised above the head with both hands, elbows bent, body slightly crouched, weight distributed evenly between both feet. The staff is positioned vertically over the head, ready to strike downward.
persons.tactic: This is the 'upper section posture' (上段之構), a defensive or preparatory stance for an overhead strike using a staff. It demonstrates readiness to counter an opponent’s attack from above while maintaining balance and control.

images.original_text: (二) 構之中段

persons.posture: In a side profile stance, right foot forward, left foot back. The figure holds a staff horizontally at waist level with both hands, arms extended forward, body angled slightly to the left. Weight is shifted forward onto the front leg, knees bent for stability. The staff is held parallel to the ground, aimed straight ahead.
persons.tactic: This is the 'middle section posture' (中段之構), used for mid-level strikes or blocks. It allows for quick transitions to offense or defense, maintaining a balanced center of gravity while preparing to thrust or parry at mid-height.

images.original_text:
中國古俠劍法跋
三
（精撫係側之低截）構之侵下（三）

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-stepping stance with left foot forward, body slightly crouched and angled to the side. Both hands grip a long sword horizontally across the body, blade pointing leftward, positioned low near the waist level. Weight is distributed with more pressure on the front (left) leg, back (right) leg providing support. The head is turned slightly toward the direction of the sword’s tip, eyes focused forward.
persons.tactic: This depicts a low-side cut or block technique, likely used to intercept an opponent’s weapon or strike from the side. The positioning suggests a defensive maneuver transitioning into an offensive counter-strike.

images.original_text: （四）八相之棒

persons.posture: Standing in a stable, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Holds a long staff vertically with both hands, one hand near the top, the other near the middle, staff aligned with the centerline of the body. Head is upright, gaze forward, shoulders relaxed but engaged. Body is squared to the front, weight evenly distributed between both legs.
persons.tactic: This represents a ready stance for staff combat, possibly preparing for a vertical thrust, block, or sweeping motion. The posture indicates readiness for defense or initiation of a linear attack along the staff’s axis.

images.original_text:
構 膝（五）
中國古俠劍法跋
四
「構」圖係據日本平松登喜壽劍道手引，其中「下段之構」，所執劍器，較茅志鳳頭勢，點劍勢為高，似乎不能稱之為同，然金子近次劍道學中，其「下段之構」。如（三）圖添繪所示，與鳳頭勢，點劍勢相間。

persons.posture: Standing in a low, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. The body is angled forward with weight distributed evenly on both legs. The left hand grips the hilt of the sword near the guard, while the right hand holds the blade close to the tip, extending it diagonally downward and forward at approximately 45 degrees. The sword is held horizontally across the front of the body, just below waist height, with the point directed toward the ground. The head is turned slightly to the left, eyes focused ahead, suggesting readiness for defense or counterattack.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Lower Section Posture' (下段之構) in sword technique, designed to defend against low attacks while maintaining balance and control. The positioning of the sword allows for immediate upward or lateral redirection of an opponent’s strike, consistent with classical Chinese martial arts principles of intercepting and redirecting force. The description references comparison with other stances like 'Phoenix Head Posture' and 'Pointing Sword Posture', indicating this is part of a sequence or instructional series.

images.original_text: 日劍攻殺郡位，雖亦依派別而有若干之差異，然普遍不外五處！屬於擊者，曰面之正，左，右，曰闕之左，右，曰右攬手；屬於刺者：曰昭喉，曰掩脣之正而範鞘內，均與中閣古劍法相同。魏文帝典論自序云：「予幼時學擊劍，閱簡多矣，四劍之法，惟京師為善；桓、靈之間，有虎員王越，善術，稱於京師，河東，稱於京師，河又稱其能空手入白刃，余興論則良久，謂言：『將軍法非也，余顧貴好之，又得善術。』 中國古俠劍法跋 五

persons.posture: Standing in a formal martial stance, facing forward with feet shoulder-width apart. The body is upright, weight evenly distributed. Both hands grip the hilt of a sword held vertically in front of the chest, blade pointing upward. The sword is positioned centrally, aligned with the torso’s midline. The head is slightly tilted forward, eyes focused ahead. This posture suggests readiness for defense or initiation of a strike, consistent with classical Chinese swordsmanship forms.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts a foundational sword stance from ancient Chinese martial arts, likely representing the 'Zheng Mian' (正面) position mentioned in the text — a standard offensive and defensive posture used in sword combat. The positioning indicates preparation for either thrusting (刺) or blocking (擊), depending on opponent movement. The accompanying text emphasizes the importance of precise hand placement and alignment with the body’s centerline for effective technique execution.

images.original_text: 市國術館

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance, left foot forward, right foot back; body slightly crouched with weight on front leg; right hand raised holding staff horizontally, left hand extended forward in guard position.
persons.tactic: Executing a staff strike or block, preparing to engage opponent with linear attack.

persons.posture: Kneeling on one knee, body leaning forward; right arm extended downward gripping sword hilt, left arm bent across chest; head turned toward opponent.
persons.tactic: Preparing for a low-cut or thrusting sword technique, likely targeting lower torso or legs.

persons.posture: Standing upright, feet shoulder-width apart; both hands gripping sword hilt at waist level, elbows bent; gaze fixed forward.
persons.tactic: Maintaining defensive readiness, possibly preparing for parry or counterattack with sword.

persons.posture: Standing with feet parallel, body angled slightly right; right arm extended upward holding spear vertically, left arm bent at side; head tilted upward.
persons.tactic: Spear-wielding posture for vertical thrust or defensive elevation, ready to strike or deflect incoming attacks.

persons.posture: Riding a horse in profile, left leg extended forward, right leg bent behind; right hand gripping reins, left hand holding spear pointed forward; upper body leaning slightly forward.
persons.tactic: Mounted spear charge or controlled advance, using horse mobility to gain tactical advantage.

persons.posture: Running or lunging forward with right leg extended, left leg trailing; right arm swinging back, left arm bent forward as if throwing or striking; head turned toward target.
persons.tactic: Dynamic offensive movement, possibly executing a throw, kick, or weapon strike while advancing.
